1.capsule: Co-founder and captain of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, opened in 1968 after meeting Stokely Carmichael at Garfield High School. Dixon recounts the Seattle BPP's breakfast-for-school-children program at Madrona Presbyterian Church (winter 1969), his deployment to Oakland under Bobby Seale, and the party's split between the Eldridge Cleaver armed-guerrilla faction and the community-program wing led by Seale.

2.capsule: Baptist minister born in Jackson, Mississippi in 1941 who organized the first NAACP Youth Council in Mississippi in 1955 under Medgar Evers's mentorship. Brown studied under Martin Luther King Jr. at Morehouse College in a seminar on social philosophy and pastors Third Baptist Church in San Francisco. His account threads the Emmett Till and Rev. George Lee murders of 1955 through the personalist theology King brought from Boston University.

3.capsule: SNCC field secretary from Birmingham, Alabama who joined the 1961 Freedom Rides at sixteen after the Mack Charles Parker lynching radicalized her. Avery served as project director in Hale County, Alabama and worked alongside SCLC, CORE, and the NAACP through the Selma campaign. Her testimony distinguishes self-defense from overt violence in SNCC's later Black Power period.

4.entry_subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
4.capsule: Two of the four teenage "St. Augustine Four" who refused to leave the Woolworth lunch counter on July 18, 1963 and were sent to reform school under Dr. Robert B. Hayling's NAACP Youth Council. Hamilton and Ulmer returned to demonstrate under SCLC in 1964 when Dr. King came to St. Augustine, and later worked with the ACCORD Freedom Trail organization to memorialize the foot soldiers of the St. Augustine movement.

5.capsule: St. Augustine, Florida activist and beautician who organized the campaign for the Foot Soldiers Monument over more than a decade through the city's Council on Aging and Senior Advisory Committee. Vickers attempted to integrate Grace Methodist Church during the 1964 St. Augustine campaign and worked alongside Dr. Robert B. Hayling to recover the movement history the city had suppressed.

6.capsule: Filmmaker from Deming, New Mexico and founder of KAOS Network in Leimert Park, Los Angeles, who studied film at UCLA and taught at Howard University. Caldwell's media-arts practice grew out of the L.A. Rebellion cinema movement and the Project Blowed hip-hop workshops he started in 1992. His work fuses Sankofa principles, capoeira-derived breakdance, and Pan-African cultural memory.

7.capsule: SNCC Northern Coordinator in the Atlanta office who handled Friends of SNCC fundraising and project communications from 1963 through 1966. Robinson disbursed funds to the Greenwood, McComb, and Hattiesburg projects and later worked in Baltimore neighborhood organizing, in factory labor, and with Showing Up for Racial Justice. She joined SURJ after a long arc through anti-war work and Hopkins public-health research.

8.capsule: Boston Celtics center and eleven-time NBA champion born in West Monroe, Louisiana in 1934, who refused to return to the segregationist town after his mother's death. Russell recounts his grandfather firing a birdshot-loaded shotgun to disperse a Klan crowd, his draft to the Celtics as the team that broke the NBA color line, and his refusal to define himself by the basketball career imposed on him.

9.capsule: Black journalists whose front-line coverage drove Civil Rights stories into the national press. Booker, Jet's Washington bureau chief from 1955, covered Emmett Till's funeral and the Freedom Rides; Newson, of the Tri-State Defender and the Afro-American, covered the Hoxie, Arkansas and Clinton, Tennessee school desegregations and was on the bombed Anniston bus. Together they chart the gap between Black-press grit and the eventual mainstream television coverage that shifted national opinion.

10.capsule: Son of NAACP Youth Council leader Clara Luper and a participant in the 1958 Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-ins, among the earliest organized lunch-counter sit-ins of the modern movement. Luper recalls the family's living room as the Youth Council's meeting hub and the strategist debates with Dr. Charles N. Atkins over nonviolent discipline that culminated in the desegregation of downtown Oklahoma City.

11.entry_subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
11.capsule: Music director (Guy) and longtime program director (Candie) at the Highlander Folk School in Tennessee who taught the early SNCC organizers the freedom songs at the April 1960 founding conference. The Carawans introduced "We Shall Overcome," "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize," and "We'll Never Turn Back" into the movement repertoire and worked with Septima Clark, Myles Horton, and the Sea Islands communities on Johns Island, South Carolina.

12.capsule: Port Gibson, Mississippi residents who lived through the NAACP-led boycott of white merchants beginning in 1966 that produced the landmark NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Supreme Court case in 1982. The Millers testify to the role of organizer Rudy Shields, the disarming confrontations with armed white storeowners, and the mass meetings at First African Baptist Church where the boycott was sustained over years.

13.capsule: West Helena, Arkansas SNCC supporter whose family home in Phillips County hosted SNCC field workers including Mertis Lasco beginning in 1964. Young's sharecropper parents Lazarus and Lottie Lamar opened the household to the voter-registration project after the Forrest City shootout, and Young later attended Malcolm X Liberation University. She places Worth W. Long in the West Helena chapter context.

14.capsule: Photographer who documented the Civil Rights movement in Orangeburg, South Carolina from age fourteen, including the Elloree School teachers' 1956 mass resignation, Fred H. Moore's 1956 expulsion from South Carolina State College, and the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre in which three students were killed by state troopers. Williams's images appeared in Jet through the period and form the visual record published in his books Freedom & Justice and The Orangeburg Massacre.

15.capsule: Stenographer at the NAACP national office in New York from 1948, secretary to director of branches Gloster B. Current, who took the minutes at the NAACP's annual conventions through the Brown v. Board of Education years and married Thurgood Marshall in 1955. Born in Hawaii, Suyat Marshall traveled with the LDF lawyers on the Groveland Four and Emmett Till cases and served on the board of the NAACP Legal Defense Fund.

16.capsule: Second chair of SNCC (1960-1963) from Massillon, Ohio who led the Orangeburg, South Carolina student protests at South Carolina State College and was at Shaw University's April 1960 founding conference. McDew argued against Dr. King's call for SNCC to fold into SCLC as a nonviolent body, insisting nonviolence was a tactic rather than a way of life, and helped develop the jail-no-bail strategy first tested at Rock Hill, South Carolina.

17.capsule: SNCC field secretary in Sunflower County, Mississippi who drove Fannie Lou Hamer to her first attempt to register to vote in Indianola in August 1962 and later worked in Ruleville and Greenwood. McLaurin was present in Greenwood the night Stokely Carmichael declared "Black Power" from the back of a flatbed truck during the 1966 Meredith March, and his account threads Ella Baker's grassroots-organizing creed through the Mississippi Freedom Summer.

18.capsule: SNCC's first field secretary, who arrived in Albany, Georgia in October 1961 from Rock Hill, South Carolina chain-gang time and built the Albany Movement steering committee with Charles A. Jones and Cordell Reagon. Sherrod ran the Southwest Georgia Project across Baker, Terrell, and Mitchell counties through the early 1960s and was beaten with axe handles during a voter-registration march in Newton, Baker County, Georgia.

19.capsule: Southern University student in Baton Rouge who joined the 1961 sit-ins at Bon Marché and Kress lunch counters and spent three weeks in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail through Christmas 1961 with twelve other students. Siler later worked on voter registration with Mrs. Williams in the Park neighborhood and became an editorial cartoonist and journalist for the Louisiana Weekly. His account names attorneys Lolis Elie, Robert F. Collins, and Nils R. Douglas as the legal team that defended the Baton Rouge protesters.

20.capsule: Attorney and draft speechwriter to Martin Luther King Jr. who helped prepare the I Have a Dream speech for the 1963 March on Washington and served as King's personal counsel through the Birmingham campaign and the Albany movement. Jones grew up in a Catholic boarding school for indigent Black and Indian children in Pennsylvania, and his account places King's anti-Vietnam War turn within an indivisible legacy of opposition to racism, militarism, and wealth disparity.

21.capsule: Hattiesburg, Mississippi NAACP member who pursued voter registration through repeated Circuit Clerk applications under registrar Theron Lynd in the late 1950s and early 1960s. Magee and his wife Carey were subpoenaed in 1962 to Aberdeen to confirm Clyde Kennard was alive at Parchman Farm, and Magee stood night watch at Vernon Dahmer's burned-out home after the January 1966 firebombing. He testifies to the Hattiesburg biracial education committee, the Forrest County community-action agency, and the local clandestine self-defense network known as 'the deep guns.'

22.capsule: SNCC program secretary, Howard NAG member, and Mississippi Freedom Summer Holly Springs project director who entered the movement after Emmett Till's murder. Sellers organized voter registration and the MFDP delegate selection that culminated in the 1964 Atlantic City challenge, then worked with Stokely Carmichael on the Lowndes County Freedom Organization that prefigured the Black Panther Party. He recounts the Selma response to Bloody Sunday, his three-month imprisonment after the 1968 Orangeburg massacre, and the late-1967 meeting where he and Carmichael discussed Black Power with Dr. King.

23.capsule: Sasser, Georgia native whose family church, Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Terrell County, hosted Albany Movement mass meetings until it was burned to the ground by Klansmen in 1962. Browner's older sister and father were active in the movement, and he attended the 1963 March on Washington alone by train at age sixteen before enlisting in the Army that summer.

24.capsule: Howard University NAG co-founder with Stokely Carmichael and Ed Brown, SNCC steering committee representative at the 1963 March on Washington, and Secretary-General of the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania. Cox helped draft the Lowndes County Freedom Organization's research-grounded independent-party strategy in 1965, co-founded Washington's Drum and Spear Bookstore in 1968, and organized the Bayard Rustin-Malcolm X debate at Howard's Cramton Auditorium in 1961.

25.capsule: Charter members of the Bogalusa, Louisiana chapter of the Deacons for Defense and Justice, formed after the Klan burned a cross at Robert 'Bob' Hicks's yard in 1965. Fletcher Anderson worked at the Crown Zellerbach paper mill, served as a Deacons lieutenant under president Charles R. Sims, and was arrested with two CORE workers on attempted-murder charges after defending themselves outside the Acme Cafe in 1966. The couple describes night patrols, walkie-talkie networks, the march from Bogalusa to Baton Rouge to Governor John McKeithen, and the trial that brought the first Black police officers to Bogalusa.

26.capsule: Memphis native expelled from Southern University in Baton Rouge in 1961 for civil rights activity, a case that reached the Supreme Court as Cox v. Louisiana. Bailey directed the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council (LSCRRC), served on the Berkeley, California City Council in the early 1970s, and as a Tennessee circuit court judge spearheaded the conversion of the Lorraine Motel into the National Civil Rights Museum, which opened in Memphis in 1991.

27.capsule: Howard University NAG member who helped desegregate the Glen Echo Park amusement grounds outside Washington, joined the 1961 Freedom Rides, and served forty days at Mississippi's Parchman Farm before being transferred to Hinds County jail. Diamond went on to SNCC field organizing in McComb, Mississippi and across the Pike County voter-registration project.

28.capsule: Juanita Abernathy and her daughter Donzaleigh Abernathy reflect on the life of Reverend Ralph D. Abernathy, SCLC co-founder, organizer of the Montgomery bus boycott alongside Dr. King, and the SCLC president who led the Poor People's Campaign after King's 1968 assassination. The Abernathys recount the Birmingham campaign, family life in Montgomery, and the cost of bearing the movement's frontline burden.

29.capsule: Director of SCLC's Citizenship Education Program from 1960 to 1972, which trained more than 6,000 grassroots organizers at Dorchester Academy in McIntosh, Georgia in collaboration with Septima Clark, Andrew Young, and Myles Horton's Highlander Folk School. Cotton was part of Dr. King's inner SCLC circle, accompanied him to Oslo for the 1964 Nobel ceremony, and later directed student activities at Cornell University.

30.capsule: Queens College CORE member who joined New Orleans CORE in 1960, then moved to Atlanta in 1961 to run SNCC's communications office under James Forman. Zellner edited the WATS-line dispatches during Mississippi Freedom Summer, worked with the Southern Conference Educational Fund alongside Anne and Carl Braden, and is married to Bob Zellner, the first white SNCC field secretary.

32.capsule: Hunter College graduate who taught Black history at Tougaloo College in Mississippi from 1963, worked in the COFO Jackson office during Freedom Summer 1964, and co-founded the Free Southern Theater with John O'Neal and Gilbert Moses. Derby went on to document the movement as a photographer with Southern Media, Inc. and later co-founded the Black women's photography collective.

33.capsule: Hattiesburg, Mississippi educator and Dean of Student Services at the University of Southern Mississippi who first integrated USM as an undergraduate. Holloway recounts the 1966 Vernon Dahmer firebombing in Forrest County, mass meetings at True Light, St. Paul, Mount Zion, and Mount Carmel Baptist churches, and the Hattiesburg Black community's clandestine boycott-enforcement collective known as 'the Spirit.' He served on the planning commission for the Mississippi Civil Rights Museum and on the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation.

34.capsule: Jamaican-born Howard University NAG founder who ran the SNCC Washington office during the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge at Atlantic City and the MFDP congressional-seating challenge of 1965. Thelwell later founded the W.E.B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies at UMass Amherst and co-authored Stokely Carmichael's posthumous memoir 'Ready for Revolution.' His testimony covers NAG's Project Awareness, the recruitment for Freedom Summer, and the Waveland staff retreat.

35.capsule: Founding member of the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense, recruited at Merritt College in Oakland by Huey Newton and Bobby Seale in 1966. Howard ran circulation and production of The Black Panther newspaper, defended the BPP at the 1967 Stockholm war-crimes tribunal organized by Bertrand Russell, supported the New Haven Bobby Seale-Ericka Huggins trial, and later founded the Police Accountability Clinic and Helpline (PACH) in Sonoma County.

36.capsule: Widow of Vernon Dahmer Sr., the Forrest County NAACP president and Kelly Settlement landowner who was murdered when Klansmen firebombed the Dahmer home in Hattiesburg on January 10, 1966, the day after he publicly offered to pay the poll tax for anyone wishing to register. Dahmer describes harboring SNCC and COFO workers including Hollis Watkins, Curtis Hayes, and the Ladner sisters; the 1964 Freedom Summer picnic on the family land; and the U.S. v. Lynd federal voter-registration suit in which her mother-in-law was a plaintiff.

37.capsule: Co-founder with his brother Aaron of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party in 1968, the BPP's first chapter outside California. Dixon recounts the chapter's free breakfast and busing-to-prisons programs, the Madrona neighborhood organizing, the FBI's COINTELPRO surveillance, and the 1971 split between Huey Newton and Eldridge Cleaver that divided the East and West Coast Panthers.

38.entry_subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
38.capsule: Interracial couple who married in the 1940s; Emmett W. Bassett, a Black food-science PhD trained at Penn State, taught at Howard University Medical School while Priscilla Tietjen Bassett organized Northern movement support. The Bassetts worked with A. Philip Randolph, Bayard Rustin, and Stanley Levison on the 1957 Prayer Pilgrimage for Freedom and supported Robert F. Williams and Mae Mallory through the Monroe, North Carolina self-defense controversy.

39.capsule: Sumter, South Carolina civil rights attorney who defended Friendship Junior College sit-in students in Rock Hill in 1961 and represented protesters from the 1968 Orangeburg massacre. Finney went on to become the first Black Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction, eventually serving as Chief Justice, and worked alongside NAACP attorney Matthew J. Perry Jr. and field secretary I. DeQuincey Newman.

40.capsule: Bennett College student in Greensboro, North Carolina who joined the February 1, 1960 sit-in at the F.W. Woolworth lunch counter on the second day, organized through her college president Dr. Willa B. Player and in coordination with North Carolina A&T students. Terry later co-founded and chaired the W.E.B. Du Bois Department of Afro-American Studies at UMass Amherst under Sterling A. Brown's mentorship.

41.capsule: SNCC field worker from Itta Bena, Mississippi who was arrested with Fannie Lou Hamer, Annelle Ponder, and June Johnson at the Winona Trailways bus station in June 1963 returning from a Septima Clark Citizenship School session in South Carolina. Simpson shared a jail cell with Hamer through the night of the beating and continued organizing voter registration in Greenwood and across the Delta with Lawrence Guyot, Hollis Watkins, and Worth Long.

42.capsule: Black Panther Party newspaper writer based in Oakland 1972-74 who covered Bobby Seale's mayoral campaign and apprenticed under editor-in-chief David Graham Du Bois. Hopkins came up in Danville, Virginia, where his family witnessed the 1963 demonstrations against segregated stores and the library, and his account traces COINTELPRO's role in his Winston-Salem recruitment as well as the party's internal disintegration after 1973.

43.capsule: Jackson SNCC and NAACP Youth Council marcher who joined the May-June 1963 high school walkouts in response to the Pearlena Lewis Woolworth's sit-in beatings, and was held with hundreds of others at the Mississippi State Fairgrounds livestock pens that Medgar Evers called a concentration camp. After moving north in 1967 she helped organize a Black Panther Party branch in White Plains, NY, then went underground for four years following a 1971 incident before clearing the charge through a lawyer.

44.capsule: Greenwood, Mississippi native whose father organized with the NAACP and whose family hosted SNCC field secretaries Sam Block, Willie Peacock, and Stokely Carmichael during the 1962-63 Leflore County voter-registration drive. Greene worked the McComb summer project of 1964 (her freedom house was bombed days after the staff vote to bring in volunteers), the Atlantic City MFDP challenge, and the Atlanta SNCC office under James Forman and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson until 1968.

45.capsule: Twelve-year-old leader of a children's marching group jailed for five days during the 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade after Bull Connor spat on him at the steps of City Hall. Hrabowski went on to attend the funeral for the four girls killed at Sixteenth Street Baptist (one of whom, Cynthia Wesley, was his Ullman High School classmate), earned a math PhD, and founded the Meyerhoff Scholars Program at UMBC, which produces Black PhDs in science and engineering at scale.

46.entry_subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
46.capsule: Family interview anchored on the late Gayle Jenkins, founding member of the Bogalusa Voters League alongside A.Z. Young, R.T. Young, and Bob Hicks during the 1965 Louisiana movement that brought CORE and the Deacons for Defense into open conflict with the Klan. Geraldine (Gayle's sister) and Toni (her daughter) describe Gayle filing the Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board desegregation suit and refusing Governor John McKeithen's reported $50,000 buyout offer; Chuck recounts the racial violence he absorbed as one of the first Black students to integrate Bogalusa Junior High in 1967.

47.capsule: Tongva elder and founding Brown Berets member in East Los Angeles in the late 1960s, recruited through the La Piranya Coffee House. Arellanes helped open the Brown Berets free clinic and built coalitions with the Black Panther Party and Maulana Karenga's US Organization, including a Watts-rebellion-era non-aggression pact between the Chicano and Black communities.

48.capsule: Virginia Union University student arrested in the February 1960 Richmond sit-ins at Thalhimer's department store, one of 34 students whose action followed the Greensboro sit-ins by three weeks. Raised in San Francisco and San Diego, Grinnell describes the shock of the segregated Richmond courtroom and her later career as an LAUSD expulsion counselor where her arrest record kept her unhirable in Los Angeles for years.

49.capsule: Chair of the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee (CNAC) who led the 1963-64 Cambridge, Maryland movement that won federal intervention from the Kennedy administration and the deployment of General George M. Gelston's National Guard. Richardson rejected the public-accommodations referendum strategy pushed by the NAACP, framed the movement around jobs, housing, and health care via a SNCC-supported door-to-door survey, and was pulled off the stage at the 1963 March on Washington before Dr. King's address.

50.capsule: Baker County, Georgia widow of Hosie Miller, who was shot in the back in March 1965 by a white neighbor in a dispute over a cow and died a week later; the killer was tried three times and never convicted. Miller was the first Black woman elected to the Baker County Board of Education, served until 2013, and is the mother of former USDA Georgia state director Shirley Sherrod.

51.capsule: Fifth-generation native of the Lincolnville neighborhood of St. Augustine, Florida, who as a child witnessed the Klan marches, the 1964 fire-bombings, and the integration of Excelsior Elementary School during the campaign Dr. Robert B. Hayling and Dr. King led against the city. Duncan co-founded the ACCORD Freedom Trail organization that, with Northrop Grumman funding, placed 30 historical civil-rights markers across St. Augustine and trained schoolteachers in the local movement history.

52.capsule: Direct-action chair of the Tuskegee Institute Advancement League (TIAL) and later Tuskegee student body president who organized 1,500 students into 'the march that wouldn't turn around' from Macon County to Montgomery in March 1965. Patton was at the 1961 Mother's Day Massacre mass meeting at her family's home, mentored by James Forman, and survived a mysterious 1967 car accident on the eve of the Tuskegee Black Power Conference; the experience reshaped her thinking toward grassroots-led local control rather than national delegate strategies.

53.capsule: Spelman College student who became SNCC project director in Laurel, Mississippi during Freedom Summer 1964, leading the McComb-area MFDP organizing, the Freedom School curriculum, and a 23-volunteer staff at age 19 after the senior staffer was forced out of Jones County. Simmons spent fifteen days at the Mississippi State Fairgrounds livestock pens in 1965, helped author the Atlanta Project's position paper on Black Power, and later coordinated the American Friends Service Committee's project on government surveillance documenting COINTELPRO's role in dismantling the Black Panther Party and other movements.

54.capsule: Medical Committee for Human Rights volunteer in Mississippi during Freedom Summer 1964 who, with Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr. of Tufts, secured the OEO grant that established the Mound Bayou community health center in Bolivar County in 1965-66. Geiger pioneered the community-oriented primary care model based on his earlier work at Pholela in South Africa and the Lamontville public-housing project in Boston, designing the federal community health center program from a 500-square-mile Delta base that was veto-proof because the grant flowed through a Massachusetts university.

55.capsule: Chair of San Diego CORE and the CORE Western Region in the early 1960s, leading sit-ins and coin-ins against the Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric that forced both employers to begin hiring Black workers. Brown later served as a Peace Corps deputy director in Lesotho, founded the African American Studies program at San Diego State University in 1971, and built the Black Economic Development Task Force around capital formation and Black wealth-building as the unfinished work of the civil-rights movement.

56.capsule: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund attorney who joined the LDF in 1949, co-tried the Topeka case folded into Brown v. Board of Education with Robert L. Carter, and succeeded Thurgood Marshall as director-counsel in 1961. Greenberg's tenure encompassed Title VII employment litigation, the LDF training program that placed Black lawyers including Julius Chambers and Marian Wright Edelman in Southern cities with none, and the 1971 Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education busing decision.

57.capsule: Arkansas Agricultural, Mechanical, and Normal College (Pine Bluff) student suspended in the early 1960s sit-ins against the Pine Bluff Woolworth's and the Magnolia, Arkansas dime store, where the owner locked the demonstrators inside and tear-gassed the building. Jones was raised on his father Ernest's farm in Williamsville, Arkansas, in one of the few Black landowning families in the county; his older brother Troy had earlier fought off a white mob in a forced ejection from a train at McNeil, Arkansas.

58.capsule: Montgomery, Alabama Bus Boycott walker at age 11 who joined the NAACP Youth Council under Rosa Parks's direct mentorship and co-founded the Montgomery Gospel Trio with Gladys Carter and Mary Hendrix McCants. Jones credits a Highlander Folk School workshop with the origin of the 'We Are Not Afraid' verse of We Shall Overcome, sung the night Tennessee police raided Highlander and cut the lights; she later joined Bernice Johnson Reagon to co-found the Pan-Africanist Harambee Singers in Atlanta in the late 1960s.

59.capsule: Fairfield, Alabama native and Tuskegee Institute student who left college to organize full-time with SNCC in Lowndes County, Alabama, working out of the Freedom House on voter registration through 1966. Lawson moved through the Atlanta SNCC office during the Black Power transition under Stokely Carmichael and went on to a public-broadcasting career, including senior programming leadership at PBS.

60.capsule: White Arlington, Virginia native and Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) organizer at Howard University who joined the May-June 1961 Freedom Rides into Jackson, Mississippi and was incarcerated at Parchman Farm. Mulholland desegregated Glen Echo Amusement Park in 1960 through the summer-long picket campaign with Bannockburn, Maryland community support, and later transferred to Tougaloo College in Mississippi, where she was one of the white students at the 1963 Jackson Woolworth's sit-in alongside Pearlena Lewis, Anne Moody, and John Salter.

61.capsule: Justice Department Civil Rights Division attorneys under John Doar from the early 1960s, with John prosecuting voting-rights and church-burning cases across Mississippi, Alabama, and Georgia (including the Itta Bena, Mississippi mass arrest of 1963) and Jean working as a research analyst on school desegregation, police brutality, and employment cases. The Rosenbergs left federal service in 1970 to found Appalachian Research and Defense Fund (AppalReD) in Prestonsburg, Kentucky, providing legal services on coal mine health and safety, black lung, strip mining, and consumer law across 37 Appalachian counties.

62.capsule: Olympic sprinter who, with Tommie Smith, raised a black-gloved fist on the medal stand at the 1968 Mexico City Games to call attention to the Olympic Project for Human Rights. Carlos traces his formation to a Harlem childhood on Lenox Avenue, training under Joseph "Joe" Yancey at the New York Pioneer Club, and Dr. Harry Edwards's organizing of the proposed Olympic boycott; he describes the personal cost of the protest, including his first wife's suicide and decades of professional exclusion.

63.capsule: SNCC organizer in Atlanta and southwest Georgia who later founded the Northern Student Movement Freedom Library in North Philadelphia and the Liberation Fellowship Church. Churchville also worked inside the Nation of Islam in Atlanta under Minister Jeremiah X. Pugh before returning to grassroots organizing, and his account threads movement work through later careers in law, theology, and Germantown community development.

64.capsule: Joint interview with six surviving organizers of the 1951 Adkin High School walkout in Kinston, North Carolina, in which Black students protested unequal facilities and won eight petitioned improvements within eighteen months. Charles Jarmon, Eleanor Stewart, John Dudley, Frances Suggs, Harold Suggs, and Samuel Dove recount Jim Crow tobacco-field and country-club work, the role of Dr. Alice Hannibal in shielding the action, and Samuel Dove's later organizing in CORE and SNCC across the South.

65.capsule: Voter-registration canvasser in Terrell County, Georgia who worked with SNCC field staff including Carolyn Daniels and Lucius Holloway during the early-1960s campaign in Dawson and Sasser. McCullar later served as a deputy registrar with the NAACP from 1979 until federal registration changes ended that role, and recounts the persistent intimidation that kept many sharecroppers from registering.

66.capsule: Co-founder of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference whose Warren Street Methodist Church in Mobile, Alabama hosted NAACP meetings and led the city's 1955 bus desegregation ride alongside Reverend S. M. McCree. Lowery was one of four ministers sued in the Sullivan v. New York Times libel case that reached the Supreme Court, chaired the SCLC committee that delivered the Selma-to-Montgomery march demands to Governor George Wallace, and delivered the benediction at Barack Obama's 2009 inauguration.

67.capsule: Married white Southern volunteers who joined SNCC's 1966 Southwest Georgia Project in Albany during the John Lewis-Stokely Carmichael leadership transition, then went on to Union Theological Seminary and decades of community work. Joseph Howell, raised in Belle Meade, Nashville, and Embry Howell, daughter of a Davidson College president, describe arriving at the Albany SNCC office to the greeting "go home, whitey" and navigating the changing role of white organizers as Black Power emerged.

68.capsule: Panola County, Mississippi native who registered to vote with her parents in Batesville and canvassed door-to-door for SNCC before joining the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party staff in Jackson and the Freedom Corps project in Bolivar County. Henderson worked alongside Frank Smith, Jean Wheeler Smith, and the strike-city tent community of plantation workers evicted for civil-rights activity.

69.capsule: SNCC staff member from Tarrytown, NY who left Swarthmore after working in the Cambridge, Maryland demonstrations and joined the Atlanta SNCC office, where she organized the 1965 residential Freedom School connecting northern and southern students. Richardson was arrested in the Toddle House sit-ins protesting Atlanta segregation that affected visiting Kenyan diplomat Oginga Odinga, and later worked on the Eyes on the Prize documentary series at Blackside.

70.capsule: Holmes County, Mississippi teacher and Mileston Community Center organizer who hosted SNCC field staff including Henry Lorenzi and Sue Lorenzi at her home and conducted voter-registration workshops on the Farm Security Administration land her father had acquired. Burns later served on the Holmes County school board and continued teaching French in the public schools, having been trained in foreign language at Mississippi Valley State and Delta State.

71.capsule: Retired Army Lieutenant General who entered the Army Air Corps reserves at 18 from Lower Merion High School and joined the all-Black 93rd Infantry Division in the Pacific in 1945, navigating the on-base segregation and the inter-divisional tensions between the 93rd and the Dixie Division on Morotai. Becton describes patrol duties keeping peace between the two units, his later career navigating the unwritten codes of being a senior Black officer, and ongoing relationships with Gen. Gordon R. Sullivan and Gen. Colin L. Powell.

72.capsule: Amherst College student who joined the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery campaign and was arrested in Montgomery's barricade confrontations, then served as president of the National Bar Association and led its Zimbabwe-independence advocacy adopted by the OAU and the UN General Council. Williams's Newark organizing brought him into the Abbott v. Burke school-equity litigation aftermath and the Abbott Leadership Institute at Rutgers, training parents and youth in advocacy and media skills.

73.capsule: Black Panther Party Communications Secretary whose father's foreign-aid work in India and the Philippines exposed her early to anti-colonial movements before she joined SNCC's Atlanta office in 1967 and met Eldridge Cleaver at the Fisk-Vanderbilt SNCC conference in Nashville. Cleaver organized the international defense campaign for Huey P. Newton and reflects on the FBI's COINTELPRO assault on the Panthers as a counterinsurgency response to armed, articulate Black political organizing.

74.capsule: Healthcare-workers organizer who entered movement work through Anne Braden's book The Wall Between while in high school in Metropolis, Illinois, joined the NAACP at the University of Illinois, and led the 1969-70 Pittsburgh hospital-workers strike that helped trigger Pennsylvania's coverage of hospital workers under state collective bargaining. Tillow heads the All Unions Committee for Single Payer Healthcare HR 676, organizing more than 600 union endorsements for John Conyers's Medicare-for-All bill.

75.entry_subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
75.capsule: Sisters from Palmers Crossing outside Hattiesburg, Mississippi who joined the NAACP youth chapter under Clyde Kennard and Vernon Dahmer and went on to SNCC and CORE staff work, with Joyce Ann Ladner directed by Ruby Doris Smith Robinson to work the Harlem March on Washington office under Bayard Rustin after Medgar Evers's June 1963 assassination. Their joint testimony traces movement leadership from the Emmett Till lynching through the 1964 Atlantic City challenge and recovers genealogical research linking their family to the white Ladner line of coastal Mississippi.

76.capsule: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party chairman from Pass Christian who organized in Hattiesburg, Jackson, and Greenwood during the Freedom Summer of 1964 and helped bring the Congressional Challenge that drove Section 5 of the Voting Rights Act. Guyot's account locates SNCC's strategic decisions inside the larger arc from Theodore Bilbo's Mississippi to the Lyndon B. Johnson administration's reluctant accommodation of Black political enfranchisement.

77.capsule: Port Gibson, Mississippi homemaker who housed civil rights lawyers including Iris Brest and Marian Wright Edelman's office staff during the long NAACP boycott of white merchants and joined voter-registration canvassing with Rudy Shields and Reverend Eddie Dawson. Guster recounts being followed by an armed white truck driver as she drove canvassers home and her later distance from the second boycott that targeted Black-owned businesses she felt had complied with the first.

78.capsule: Co-founder with her husband Millard Dean Fuller of Habitat for Humanity and later the Fuller Center for Housing, who entered movement work through their move to Koinonia Farm in Americus, Georgia after giving away their Montgomery business fortune. Degelmann recounts being arrested with her infant in Sumter County for visiting a Black family in defiance of a local ordinance and describes the alienation from her Tuscaloosa, Alabama family that her interracial witness produced.

79.capsule: Freedom Summer 1964 volunteer in Greenville, Mississippi who first encountered the Mississippi movement through a 1963 Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp at Tougaloo College, where she heard Fannie Lou Hamer recount the Winona beating. Todd worked the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party precinct and county conventions in Washington County and observed the friction between SNCC-trained activists and the traditional Black leadership that stepped in at the county level.

80.capsule: Co-founder of the Atlanta Student Movement and the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights at Morehouse College who led the March 1960 sit-ins coordinated across the Atlanta University Center. King traces his organizing instincts to his grandfather, Rev. Joseph P. Smith, who recruited NAACP memberships in rural Calhoun County, Georgia, and recounts Dr. Benjamin E. Mays following him back to the dormitories to witness him address three thousand students before a march despite the state troopers waiting downtown.

81.capsule: SNCC photographer and literacy-materials producer whose Mexican-American father and Irish-German mother shaped her entry into the Catholic social-inquiry movement before she joined the Selma campaign and then organized in Batesville, Mississippi. Varela trained under New Orleans photographer Matt Herron, documented the James Meredith March Against Fear and the Poor People's Campaign, transcribed Fannie Lou Hamer's first autobiography with Julius Lester, and later spent thirty years building Hispano land-based cooperatives in northern New Mexico.

82.capsule: Daughter of Clara Luper who, at age eight, made the motion to sit-in at Katz Drug Store in Oklahoma City in August 1958. Hildreth recounts the 1957 NAACP Youth Council Northern tour that catalyzed the OKC campaign, her mother's 26 arrests, and the 1969 Oklahoma City Sanitation Workers Strike.

83.capsule: SNCC field secretary who organized voter registration in Albany, Greenwood, and Selma between 1963 and 1967. A University of Michigan SDS member recruited by Tom Hayden, Norman Noonan worked under Charles Sherrod and Sam Block before relocating to Selma's ward-by-ward project; she later co-edited Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC.

84.capsule: Albany Movement office worker who staffed Martin Luther King Jr.'s office at Dr. William G. Anderson's home during the 1961-62 campaign, taking notes on church fans when she ran out of paper. Jenkins authored Open Themselves: A Pictorial History of the Albany Civil Rights Movement, drawn from mass-meeting testimony she recorded as a Carver Junior High School teacher.

85.capsule: Albany Movement participant who served on the police-liaison committee with McCree Harris that pressed Chief Laurie Pritchett to hire the city's first Black officers. Jones was arrested in the 1961 mass jailings, taught voter-registration literacy classes for the Criterion Club, and led the 1961 Black Christmas boycott that diverted shoppers from downtown Albany.

86.capsule: San Diego organizer who founded Trabajadores de la Raza, served as executive director of the Chicano Federation, and sued San Diego County to break employment barriers excluding Mexican Americans, African Americans, Filipinos, and Asian Americans. Camarillo helped draft the 1986 Immigration Reform and Control Act through the National Council of La Raza and trained as an organizer under Saul Alinsky on a Ford Foundation fellowship.

87.capsule: South Carolina civil rights attorney and later US District Judge who tried hundreds of segregation cases as NAACP Legal Defense Fund cooperating counsel from the early 1950s onward. Perry witnessed the 1947 Elmore v. Rice and Wrighten v. Board of Trustees trials in Judge J. Waties Waring's Columbia courtroom, attended the Briggs v. Elliott trial in 1951, and co-led the Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company bus-desegregation case that preceded Browder v. Gayle.

88.capsule: Petersburg, Virginia-raised Methodist who became active in the Methodist Student Movement and was elected to its statewide office as a high-school senior. Moore describes growing up in a segregated mill town where his home church bears a plaque marking the 1846 founding site of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South over the issue of slavery.

89.capsule: Chicago Black Panther Party member in the Illinois chapter's education cadre under Deputy Chairman Fred Hampton. McCarty was expelled from Saint Ignatius College Prep in March 1969 for organizing a Black student walkout, joined the BPP that fall, and recounts the Original Rainbow Coalition formed with the Young Lords and Young Patriots Organization before COINTELPRO infiltration ended Hampton's life.

90.capsule: NAACP national staff member from 1953 who began selling subscriptions to The Crisis magazine at age nine in Haywood County, Tennessee, where her parents organized the first local NAACP chapter. Roxborough rose through the New York office as field secretary, executive assistant to Roy Wilkins, and eventually director of programs; she worked on Hoxie school desegregation, the ACT-SO youth program, and the 1955 Emmett Till mobilization.

91.capsule: Louisiana-born Los Angeles teacher and CORE member who tested California's 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act by integrating motels and picketed housing tracts in Los Angeles for fair employment. Walter became a children's book author whose works-including Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World and Mississippi Challenge-won the Coretta Scott King Award, Jane Addams Children's Book Award, and Christopher Award.

92.capsule: Centenarian Claiborne County, Mississippi farmer who joined the NAACP underground in the early 1950s, paid poll tax for years before successfully registering to vote, and led the Port Gibson NAACP branch during the 1966 boycott that produced the Claiborne Hardware Co. v. NAACP Supreme Court case. Jones recounts attending Bob Moses voter-registration classes during R.L.T. Smith's congressional campaign and serving as a shareholder-board member of the cooperative Mart grocery store.

93.capsule: Los Angeles Black Panther Party member who at age nineteen coordinated the Alprentice "Bunchy" Carter People's Free Clinic in South Central, then directed the George Jackson Free Clinic in Berkeley and became Minister of Finance under Elaine Brown. Mtume later co-founded SHIELDS for Families, which grew to twenty-five million in funding serving women with substance-use disorders and prenatally-exposed children at Charles R. Drew University of Medicine and Science.

94.capsule: Son of NAACP Legal Defense Fund attorney Oliver W. Hill Sr., who was among the first Black students to desegregate Richmond, Virginia's public schools in the early 1960s under the city's pupil placement board. A Howard University graduate and Virginia State University psychology professor, Hill works on Black educational achievement and collaborates with Bob Moses's Algebra Project in Petersburg, Virginia.

96.capsule: Hattiesburg, Mississippi beautician and MFDP executive secretary who collected poll tax for her customers, taught citizenship classes after training at Highlander Folk School's Dorchester Center, and was the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the redistricting suit that produced Mississippi's first Black congressional district. Connor attended the 1964 Atlantic City convention as an MFDP delegate and traveled to Washington for the 1965 congressional challenge against the seating of Mississippi's white representatives.

97.capsule: Folk musician who opened for Paul Robeson at the 1949 Peekskill concerts attacked by the American Legion and Ku Klux Klan, and helped popularize "We Shall Overcome" after learning it from Zilphia Horton at Highlander Folk School in 1946. Seeger sang at Highlander's 1957 anniversary alongside Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, and Rosa Parks, and joined the 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery march and Mississippi Freedom Summer.

98.capsule: Cleveland-born SNCC organizer who worked simultaneously in SDS and SNCC, going to Newark in December 1964 to organize in the Black community. Hutchings was active in SNCC through the Black Power period after Stokely Carmichael's 1967 chairmanship transition and worked on building the Black United Front in Washington, DC.

99.capsule: St. Augustine, Florida teenager who joined Dr. Robert B. Hayling's 1964 demonstrations after being humiliated as a child by a white employer who served him beside a dog's food tray. Conway was beaten by a white mob at St. Augustine Beach during a wade-in alongside CORE worker Al Lingo, then later integrated New York City Police Department promotional exams through a successful federal lawsuit that became precedent nationwide.

100.entry_subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
100.capsule: Branch and Elaine Armstrong (the latter not interviewed here) desegregated the University of Southern Mississippi in 1965; Branch later worked as the first Black employee hired at Hattiesburg's telephone company and Sears under stealth NAACP coordination. Smith, of Jones County's mixed-race "free state of Jones" Newton Knight descent, organized food distribution for SNCC workers during Mississippi Freedom Summer alongside Vernon Dahmer and JC Fairley and watched her husband Dr. Charles Smith integrate Forrest General Hospital.

101.capsule: Baltimore-born Civic Interest Group organizer who joined SNCC in 1961 and went to McComb, Mississippi to work with Bob Moses on voter registration. Robinson was among the first SNCC field secretaries dispatched to Mississippi after the Louisville SNCC meeting voted to combine voter registration with direct action.

102.capsule: Shreveport, Louisiana minister who joined Martin Luther King Jr.'s SCLC staff in March 1960 and was beaten unconscious by police inside Little Union Baptist Church on September 22, 1963, during a memorial service for the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing victims. As pastor of Mount Canaan Baptist Church since 1966, Blake built 120 units of low-income housing and a senior tower for the Allendale community and now runs an in-church homeschool for Black boys struggling with literacy.

103.capsule: St. Augustine, Florida dentist and NAACP Youth Council advisor who led the 1963-64 St. Augustine Movement that helped force passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Hayling describes being beaten unconscious at the September 1963 Klan rally with Hoss Manucy's Klansmen, the recruiting of Yale Divinity students under Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr., and Mary Peabody's arrest at the Monson Motor Lodge.

104.capsule: New Orleans civil rights attorney with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee who litigated Title VII discrimination cases against the Crown Zellerbach paper mill on behalf of the Black union in Bogalusa, Louisiana. Sobol partnered with the Black firm Collins, Douglas, and Elie (CORE's Louisiana attorneys) on school-desegregation, voting-rights, and Duncan v. Perez cases before federal judges including Frederick J.R. Heebe and Herbert W. Christenberry.

105.capsule: Wesleyan student who joined SNCC's 1963 Atlanta lunch-counter sit-ins and was dispatched with Bruce Gordon and Joe Iyer to support Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark's Chatham County Crusade for Voters night marches in Savannah, Georgia. Tuttle later infiltrated white-supremacist meetings on the SCLC team's behalf and, as Los Angeles City Controller from 1985, led the divestment from companies doing business in apartheid South Africa.

106.capsule: High Point, North Carolina public-relations strategist who served as corporate-integration intermediary between SCLC and the Woolworth's national retail chain, opening lunch counters and executive offices to Black hires after the 1960 sit-ins. Brown coordinated the Memphis Peabody Hotel desegregation test for Dr. King and Wyatt Tee Walker, joined the Nixon White House through Bob Finch, and later arranged Coretta Scott King's 1986 visit to South Africa to press P.W. Botha for Nelson Mandela's release.

107.capsule: First African American elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives in the twentieth century, winning the Holmes County seat as an independent in 1967 with Marian Wright as his lead lawyer through the contested seating. A descendant of slaves on the same plantation where he still lived, Clark served continuously, became Speaker Pro Tempore in 1992, and was part of the cohort that grew to 35 Black legislators by 2000.

108.capsule: NAACP Legal Defense Fund attorney who joined Thurgood Marshall's staff in 1944 after clerking for William H. Hastie and helped engineer the strategic shift in June 1950 from equalization suits to a direct attack on Plessy v. Ferguson. Carter argued the McLaurin v. Oklahoma graduate-school case and was central to the litigation strategy that produced Brown v. Board of Education in 1954.

109.capsule: Albany Movement field worker recruited by Charles Sherrod into the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project, who organized voter-registration work and confronted school boards in Newton and surrounding counties. McClary describes a near-fatal assault at the SNCC office that was broken up by another staff member, framing his account around self-defense, refusal to retreat, and the eventual election of Newton's first Black mayor.

110.capsule: Black Panther Party member at the Oakland national headquarters in 1969, recruited out of Berkeley after participating in Los Angeles Woolworth's demonstrations as a high-school student. Daughter of an interracial couple raised in Adams Boulevard Los Angeles, Alexander taught at UCSD's Third College (later Thurgood Marshall College) and continues community-organizing work with San Diego's Pillars of the Community on policing and incarceration.

111.capsule: Holmes County, Mississippi Movement worker at the Mileston community center who staffed the Child Development Group of Mississippi Head Start program after the Medical Committee for Human Rights established the on-site clinic with Dr. Albert Versailles. Head describes the federal defunding of CDGM under pressure from Senator John C. Stennis, the lobbying bus trip to Washington that secured Head Start funding, and the Tougaloo coursework she completed while running the center.

112.capsule: Tuskegee Institute student and SNCC worker in Lowndes County, Alabama whose life was saved on August 20, 1965 when Episcopal seminarian Jonathan Daniels pulled her behind him before Tom Coleman's shotgun blast killed him at a Hayneville, Alabama country store. Sales testified at Coleman's acquittal, was the chief eyewitness to the Father Richard Morrisroe shooting moments later, and went on to Princeton and Manhattanville before founding the SpiritHouse Project.

113.capsule: Americus, Georgia high-school SNCC organizer who joined Don Harris, John Perdew, and Ralph Allen at the local SNCC house from late 1962 onward and helped publish the mimeographed Voice of Americus newsletter. Mahone documents the Leesburg Stockade detention of juvenile girls in an abandoned prison camp and convenes the biennial Americus Movement Remembered tribute that has been collecting photographs, oral histories, and newspaper clippings since 2007.

114.capsule: Worth County, Sylvester, Georgia organizer with the Southwest Georgia Project under Charles Sherrod who was mentored by Randolph Battle and Robert Mants during the freedom-of-choice school-integration period. Young attended SNCC-sponsored leadership training including a New York Synectics communications-skills program and integrated white schools in Worth County.

115.capsule: Pastor of Mount Zion Baptist Church in Seattle who hosted Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1961 Seattle visit at the First Presbyterian Church after another venue cancelled. A Morehouse College and Colgate Rochester Theological Seminary graduate from the same generation as King, McKinney spent decades organizing Pacific Northwest civil-rights work and received a written apology from First Presbyterian in 1998 for the original cancellation.

116.capsule: French literature professor at the University of the South in Sewanee, Tennessee who joined the Highlander Folk School board in 1958 and served continuously through the school's relocation and renaming as the Highlander Research and Education Center. A World War II Omaha Beach veteran and Carleton College graduate, Bates was board president during the 1960s when Highlander hosted SNCC and SCLC strategy sessions and connected the civil rights movement to later organizing on gender and sexual orientation.

117.capsule: Baker County, Georgia organizer who joined the Federation of Southern Cooperatives after her father was murdered by a white neighbor in 1965 and co-founded New Communities Inc., a Black agricultural land trust with her husband Charles Sherrod. Her Pigford v. Glickman discrimination claim against USDA reached $12 million in 2009 after a Bush-era hearing officer initially ruled against the family; the settlement funded the purchase of Cypress Pond Plantation, formerly owned by Georgia's wealthiest slaveholder.

118.capsule: CORE freedom-rider from New York who was arrested and beaten in a Houston jail's white-male misdemeanor tank for challenging segregated coffee-shop service en route to Jackson, Mississippi. McNichols subsequently worked with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and observed firsthand the AFL-CIO and NAACP leadership's anti-communist hostility toward SNCC's young organizers.

119.capsule: CORE field secretary who, as a graduate of South Carolina's Claflin University, helped plan the 1961 Freedom Rides architecture before the Anniston bus burning and was jailed during the 1959 Miami CORE Action Institute sit-ins. Gaither worked in Mississippi alongside Medgar Evers and Kentucky and California CORE projects before earning a botany Ph.D. and joining the Slippery Rock, Pennsylvania faculty.

120.capsule: SNCC strategist and Howard University Law School alumnus who organized the 1961 Fisk University voter-registration seminar that he calls the first Freedom School, drawing in Stokely Carmichael, Charles McDew, and Ella Baker. Jenkins later served on the US Postal Service Board of Governors under President Carter, co-chaired the National Conference of Black Lawyers, and served on Howard University and University of the District of Columbia boards of trustees.

121.capsule: Son of NAACP voter-registration leader Vernon F. Dahmer Sr., who was murdered by Sam Bowers's White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan on January 10, 1966 when Klansmen firebombed the family home and grocery store in the Kelly Settlement north of Hattiesburg, Mississippi. Dahmer Jr. led the family campaign that secured Bowers's conviction in 1998, thirty-two years after the killing, after earlier prosecutions through 1968 had produced only five convictions.

122.capsule: Virginia Union University student who participated in the Richmond, Virginia sit-in movement targeting the Thalhimer's department store's whites-only Richmond Room, and was arrested and spat on by a white man after release. Her father, a Newport News postman, refused to let her board the Mississippi-bound buses; George later did anti-poverty counseling in Washington, D.C. and built a career in corporate diversity and transgender-inclusion work.

123.capsule: Holmes County, Mississippi contractor and gospel singer who became Durant movement leader and Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party chair, organizing the county-wide meetings that picked MFDP delegates for the 1964 Atlantic City convention. Bruce describes county-wide voter-registration drives, the Mileston Fourteen, the firebombing of Hartman Turnbow's house, his own jailing during the J.J. Young protest in Lexington, and integrating Durant's lunch counters, drugstores, and the city school board after Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education. Worked alongside SNCC, Sue and Henry Lorenzi at the Mileston community center, and Robert Clark's 1967 campaign for the Mississippi State Legislature.

124.capsule: Bronx-raised son of former Young Communist League parents who joined the sit-in movement at Harpur College in 1960 and ran SNCC's Washington DC office during the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge to the regular Democratic delegation in Atlantic City. Tillow recounts coordinating with Ella Baker, Bayard Rustin, James Forman, and credentials-committee allies including Joseph Rauh, Edith Green, and Robert Kastenmeier to secure a roll-call vote on the MFDP's minority report. After leaving SNCC he worked for the United Electrical Workers in Pittsburgh and the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists allied causes through the 1990s.

125.capsule: First cousin of Emmett Till and eyewitness to his August 1955 kidnapping from the Wright family home in Money, Mississippi, where Parker, age 16, was sleeping in an adjacent room when J.W. Milam and Roy Bryant arrived after midnight. Parker describes the visit to Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market, the terror of the kidnapping night, his return alone by train through Memphis to Argo, Illinois, and his decades of public testimony at Mississippi Freedom Trail markers and to school audiences.

126.capsule: Albany, Georgia physician who served as first president of the Albany Movement in 1961-62, inviting Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and SCLC into the campaign that filled the jails of southwest Georgia. Anderson describes growing up in segregated Americus, the Criterion Club's organizing role, his eventual professional rapprochement with Police Chief Laurie Pritchett, and his subsequent decades convening the Visiting Minority Faculty Series at Michigan State University. He cites Wyatt Tee Walker, Joseph E. Lowery, Shirley Sherrod, and Juanita Abernathy as movement colleagues he has hosted.

127.capsule: Boston-raised Northern Student Movement organizer and longtime UMass Amherst Africana Studies professor who produced the CBS television series Black Heritage: A History of Afro-Americans with Vincent Harding and John Henrik Clarke. Strickland recalls personal acquaintance with Malcolm X dating to Roxbury before his Harvard speech, working on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Atlantic City Challenge, and his later writing on Frantz Fanon, the Black Panthers, and the limits of electoral politics.

128.capsule: AFSCME international secretary-treasurer who helped lead the 1968 Memphis sanitation workers' strike, co-authoring the slogan I AM A MAN with Rev. James Lawson after sanitation worker meetings at the Peabody Hotel. Lucy co-founded the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists in 1972 and led the Free South Africa Movement starting in 1984, instrumental in Nelson Mandela's 1990 American tour following his release from prison.

129.capsule: White Jewish SCOPE volunteer from California who worked in Macon, Georgia under William P. Randall in summer 1965 organizing voter registration through black barbershops, pool halls, and the office that later became Phil Walden's Capricorn Records. Leventhal was arrested by Macon police and subsequently spent two months in Los Angeles jail psychiatric custody after a 1967 confrontation with the LAPD, an experience that shaped his later civil-rights litigation against the qualified-immunity doctrine.

130.capsule: Johns Island, South Carolina-born civil rights organizer who helped lead the 1969 Charleston Hospital Workers Strike alongside five fired Black nurses, the Drug, Hospital and Health Care Employees union, and SCLC. Saunders recounts the role of Black Muslim leader Otis Robinson in crafting the You Are Somebody message later associated with Jesse Jackson, his work to win statutory recognition of Gullah as a language, and his founding of the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program in 1970.

131.capsule: Durham, North Carolina-raised son of an AME Zion Church presiding elder who served as a SNCC field secretary and was known as the poet of SNCC, originator of the quote false-face America, we have found you out. Long discusses growing up in the Hayti district near North Carolina College, the AME Zion network of churches under his father, family lawyer Floyd McKissick, and the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church's structural ties to NAACP chapter formation across his father's Durham district.

132.capsule: SCLC chief of staff under Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and chief architect of Project C, the strategic plan for the 1963 Birmingham campaign that produced the Letter from Birmingham Jail, which Walker transcribed from King's manuscript fragments. Walker had previously served as pastor of Gillfield Baptist Church in Petersburg, Virginia, where he led a 1960 library integration arrest, and afterward served as Southern Regional Coordinator for the March on Washington and as pastor of Canaan Baptist Church in Harlem for 37 years.

133.capsule: Bronx-raised physician and son of Hungarian-Jewish immigrants who co-founded the Medical Committee for Human Rights in 1964 and served as its first secretary-treasurer, raising half a million dollars in two years. Moldovan went to Meridian, Mississippi during Freedom Summer 1964, present when the bodies of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner were recovered, and helped arrange the independent autopsy by Dr. David Spain that contradicted the official Mississippi finding.

134.capsule: SCLC executive staff member and longtime King lieutenant who staged a sit-in in Peoria, Illinois in 1947, thirteen years before Greensboro, and later joined the 1961 Freedom Rides on the Montgomery-to-Jackson bus leg under armed Alabama and Mississippi National Guard escort. Vivian describes the Nashville nonviolent training under James Lawson, the Red Sea March of Birmingham parents to the jail in May 1963, and his confrontation with Sheriff Jim Clark on the Selma courthouse steps in 1965.

135.entry_subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
135.capsule: Chicago Theological Seminary classmates and later spouses who drove to Selma in March 1965 after Bloody Sunday in response to Dr. King's call, joining the Jesse Jackson-organized seminary delegation. David, a rural Illinois white seminarian, and Satoko, a Japanese student educated at Kobe College and Yankton College, lodged in Selma's Black community where host families stood overnight guard outside their bedrooms; David later left seminary for law school and the National Council of Churches' Washington anti-poverty advocacy.

136.capsule: Madison, Illinois-born organizer raised on an 88-acre cotton farm in Marvell, Arkansas, who founded the Boys, Girls, and Adults Community Development Center (BGACDC) in Marvell and directed Head Start outreach across the rural Delta. Jackson describes voter-registration work during the Mississippi summer killings, her husband carrying his pistol through hostile white-owned land, and home-visit reading programs aimed at every child seeing a book before entering school.

137.capsule: Washington DC-raised Dunbar High School and Howard University graduate who served as a SNCC field worker in Marvell, Arkansas from 1965 to 1967, partnered with Jewish co-worker Howard Himmelbaum on welfare-rights workshops and community organizing. Glascoe discusses the 1966 SNCC decision that white workers should leave Black communities for organizing in white communities, and her subsequent career as an education theorist at Gettysburg College critiquing what she calls the failure of desegregated schools to prepare Black children.

138.capsule: Younger cousin of Emmett Till who at age 12 shared the bedroom in Money, Mississippi from which J.W. Milam and Roy Bryant kidnapped Till on the night of August 28, 1955, after Till's visit to Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market. Wright's father Mose Wright testified at the Sumner murder trial against Milam and Bryant, an act of identification by a Black man in a Mississippi courtroom that forced the family's relocation to Argo, Illinois. Wright published Simeon's Story in 2010 to correct documentary inaccuracies about the case.

139.capsule: Hattiesburg, Mississippi student who desegregated the city's schools as part of an NAACP-recruited cohort, entering Tims junior high while her younger sister integrated the elementary grades. Funchess recounts isolation tactics used by teachers (separate class periods from other Black students, forced solo seating) and recalls hearing Martin Luther King Jr. speak in Hattiesburg about the Poor People's Campaign weeks before his assassination. She tracks the city's school consolidation through the Alexander v. Holmes ruling and the racial composition of Hattiesburg's graduating class of 1972.

140.capsule: Albany, Georgia resident who worked as a maid and later as an emergency medical technician in Baker County after completing EMT training in 1983. Broadway recounts the Albany Movement era, including the "Bloody Saturday" attacks on students and Dr. William Anderson Shirley's practice of treating injured demonstrators without charge as his contribution to the movement.

141.entry_subject: Lucius Holloway, Sr., and Emma Kate Holloway
141.capsule: Albany, Georgia movement participants whose activism cost Lucius Holloway his postal service job after he was fired on contested grounds. Lucius served as first vice president of the local NAACP beginning in 1955, succeeding to leadership after two successive presidents died, one killed in office. Emma Kate Holloway recounts the family's farming origins, the household economy that sustained their organizing, and the couple's continued work through repeated retaliation.

142.capsule: SNCC field worker in the Mississippi Delta who served as a driver and rigged cars with kill-switches on the tail lights to evade nighttime pursuers on rural roads. Zapata worked alongside Fannie Lou Hamer and Susie Ruffin, whom he describes as the backbone of local organizing to which incoming SNCC staff deferred. He later relocated to Washington, D.C., and traveled through Mexico and Central and South America, engaging with indigenous religious traditions.
